title: Twilight of the Idols - Maxims on Strength and Self-Respect
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use for concise Nietzschean challenge around action, self-respect, and courage.
misreading_risk: Can be flattened into motivational one-liners.
chunk_text: TITLE: Maxims on Strength and Self-Respect WORK: Twilight of the Idols SECTION: Maxims and Missiles, selected excerpt AUTHOR: Friedrich Nietzsche THEMES: strength, action, self-respect, courage, affirmation SOURCE_TYPE: text Even the pluckiest among us has but seldom the courage of what he really knows. Once for all I wish to be blind to many things.-Wisdom sets bounds even to knowledge. A man recovers best from his exceptional nature-his intellectuality-by giving his animal instincts a chance. That which does not kill me, makes me stronger. Help thyself, then everyone will help thee. A principle of neighbour-love. A man should not play the coward to his deeds. He should not repudiate them once he has performed them. Pangs of conscience are indecent. If

chunk_text: man should not play the coward to his deeds. He should not repudiate them once he has performed them. Pangs of conscience are indecent. If a man knows the wherefore of his existence, then the manner of it can take care of itself. Man does not aspire to happiness; only the Englishman does that. One should adopt only those situations in which one is in no need of sham virtues, but rather, like the tight-rope dancer on his tight rope, in which one must either fall or stand-or escape. I distrust all systematisers, and avoid them. The will to a system, shows a lack of honesty. Only those thoughts that come by walking have any value. What matters it whether

And he who laughs best to-day, will also laugh last. The formula of my happiness: a Yea, a Nay, a straight line, goal.
